query: when to use promises instead of async await javascript

selection.greatFrontendReasonIfOmitted: GreatFrontEnd has Promise fundamentals material, but not a directly matching page about when direct Promise style is preferable to async/await.

competitors.label: MDN Using promises
competitors.selectionReason: MDN is the strongest canonical competitor for Promise chaining, composition, and concurrency combinators.
competitors.winReason: MDN remains the broader reference, but our page now wins this decision-oriented comparison because it pairs the accidental-serialization pitfall with shared in-flight work and a direct rule of thumb for choosing raw Promise style.
theirStrengths: It explains Promise chains, composition, and combinators with strong reference coverage.
theirGaps: It is not explicitly organized around the decision question of when raw Promise style is preferable to async/await.

realWorldUseCases: The common production pitfall is hiding independent work behind sequential await calls and accidentally adding latency.
followUpConfusion: Use async/await when the main thing you are expressing is a story-like sequence of business steps. Use raw Promise style when the main thing you are expressing is the relationship between async tasks: start together, dedupe, race, join, or hand one in-flight result to multiple callers.

realWorldUseCases: MDN explains Promise composition well, but our page is more explicitly framed around the product decision of when Promise style is the better tool.
actionableExamples: MDN still has strong composition examples, so the actionable-example comparison stays close.
followUpConfusion: Our page now answers the false-dichotomy and 'when exactly' follow-up more directly.

actionableExamples: Add one tiny allSettled or race example if we want a clearer code-level edge over MDN's broader combinator reference.

competitors.label: DEV createSharedPromise
competitors.selectionReason: This article is a strong real-world example of Promise-based in-flight sharing, which is one of our core use cases.
competitors.winReason: The DEV article is strong on one dedupe abstraction, but our page now wins the broader decision question by pairing that use case with a sequential-await vs Promise.all comparison and an explicit rule of thumb.
theirStrengths: It has a very concrete in-flight deduplication example built around a reusable Promise abstraction.
theirGaps: It is narrower and does not cover the broader decision space of composition and combinators.

realWorldUseCases: A product page needs recommendations, inventory, and pricing from separate APIs. Running them sequentially with await adds unnecessary latency, while Promise combinators make fan-out/fan-in concurrency explicit. Meanwhile, product detail fetches may need one shared in-flight Promise so two widgets do not trigger duplicate requests.
The DEV article has one strong production use case around request deduplication, but our page now covers both shared in-flight work and fan-out/fan-in concurrency.
actionableExamples: Its createSharedPromise example is concrete, while our page now adds both the dedupe cache and the side-by-side concurrency example.
followUpConfusion: The article stays focused on one utility pattern, so the broader follow-up comparison remains close rather than clearly behind.
Add one note on error-reset vs stale-cache policy if we want a sharper follow-up edge on shared-Promise caching.

competitors.label: Stack Overflow async/await vs promises
competitors.selectionReason: This thread is a direct community-Q&A competitor for the exact comparison users often search.
competitors.winReason: Our page is more editorially cohesive on composition, in-flight sharing, and the false Promise-vs-async/await dichotomy than the scattered thread format.
theirStrengths: It surfaces the common point that Promise.all-style concurrency is a real reason to stay with raw Promises.
theirGaps: It is thread-style Q&A, so the explanation is narrower and less structured than a dedicated editorial page.

realWorldUseCases: The common production pitfall is hiding independent work behind sequential await calls and accidentally adding latency.
followUpConfusion: Strong interview answer: it is not Promise vs async/await as enemies; async/await is built on Promises. You choose the style that best expresses the control flow.

realWorldUseCases: The Stack Overflow thread mentions concurrency and Promise.all, but our page gives a more complete production framing around latency and shared work.
actionableExamples: The examples are practical enough that the code-example comparison remains close.
followUpConfusion: Our page answers the false-dichotomy follow-up more directly than the scattered answers do.

actionableExamples: Add one tiny Promise.race or Promise.allSettled example if we want a stronger code-surface edge over general Q&A threads.
